step_by_step_thinking:
To determine if there's statistically significant difference in the rate of extubation failures between the two groups (CPAP conventional vs Bubble CPAP), we need a statistical test suitable for comparing proportions from independent samples. Given the binary nature of success/failure events (extubation either succeeds or fails), and since the sample sizes are large enough (\(n = 160\) per group), a chi-square test for independence would be well-suited here. A paired t-test isn't applicable because the measurements aren’t taken on the same subjects over time but rather different subjects receiving one treatment versus another. Similarly, student \(t\)-test requires normally distributed continuous variables, whereas our variable (success/failed extubations) is categorical. Lastly, ANOVA assumes multiple groups comparison where all comparisons share common variance - which doesn’t fit our scenario given only two groups being compared.
Therefore, my answer is \(\boxed{B}\).

To determine if there's statistically significant difference in the rate of extubation failures between the two groups (CPAP conventional vs Bubble CPAP), we need a statistical test suitable for comparing proportions from independent samples. Given the binary nature of success/failure events (extubation either succeeds or fails), and since the sample sizes are large enough (\(n = 160\) per group), a chi-square test for independence would be well-suited here. A paired t-test isn't applicable because the measurements aren’t taken on the same subjects over time but rather different subjects receiving one treatment versus another. Similarly, student \(t\)-test requires normally distributed continuous variables, whereas our variable (success/failed extubations) is categorical. Lastly, ANOVA assumes multiple groups comparison where all comparisons share common variance - which doesn’t fit our scenario given only two groups being compared.
Therefore, my answer is \(\boxed{B}\).
